event_blocks.summary: An upbeat pop song with female vocals introduces a 'Top 10 Rio Olympics Swimming Races' title card, followed by a call-to-action for the 'OhSean Swims' channel. The focus shifts to the '10. Men's 100m Backstroke' at the brightly lit Rio 2016 Pool, set to motivational electronic pop music. Swimmers, including Ryan Murphy in lane 4 and a Romanian swimmer in a white 'ROU' cap, prepare for the race. A commentator notes Ryan Murphy's narrow semi-final victory over David Plummer by 1/100th of a second and their close proximity in the current race. The race commences with a dive, amplified by loud crowd cheers and splashing. Underwater and overhead views showcase powerful backstroke techniques. The commentator observes David Plummer's strong start in the middle lanes, marked by yellow lane ropes. Approaching the 50m mark, split times appear on-screen, indicating 'WR SPLIT 25.35 -0.17' and '50M', alongside rankings for M. Larkin (AUS), Xu J. (CHN), and D. Plummer (USA). The commentator adds that Ryan Murphy was not in medal contention at the halfway turn, with Mitchell Larkin of Australia leading, all underscored by continuous, intense crowd cheering and splashing.

Xu J..description: Chinese swimmer in lane 2, identified by on-screen graphics during the 50m split, ranked 2nd with a time of +0.08 behind the leader.

M. Larkin.description: Australian swimmer, identified by on-screen graphics during the 50m split, ranked 3rd with a time of +0.08 behind the leader. A commentator identifies him by name as Mitchell Larkin of Australia, stating he was leading at the halfway turn.

D. Plummer.description: American swimmer, identified by on-screen graphics during the 50m split, ranked 5th with a time of +0.17 behind the leader. A commentator mentions he was 1/100th of a second slower than Ryan Murphy in the semi-finals, was swimming side-by-side with Murphy, and made a stronger start in this race.

A commentator mentions he was 1/100th of a second faster than David Plummer in the semi-finals, was swimming side-by-side with Plummer, but wasn't in a medal position at the halfway turn.

event_blocks.summary: The Men's 100m Backstroke final at the Rio 2016 Olympics commenced with swimmers mid-race, the roar of the crowd and splashing water filling the air. Ryan Murphy, initially trailing, executed a decisive 'incredible turn' to take the lead, a strategy previously employed in the semi-finals. Despite Xu J. and M. Larkin leading at the 50m mark, Murphy surged in the latter half, swimming near world record pace, leaving competitors unable to respond. The Omega Timer registered his victory in 51.97 seconds, a new Olympic record and narrowly missing the world record by 3/100ths of a second. Murphy finished first, followed by Xu J. and D. Plummer. Post-race, Murphy celebrated in the pool before the victory ceremony where he received his gold medal, with Xu J. earning silver and D. Plummer taking bronze. Uplifting music accompanied Murphy on the podium with Xu J. and D. Plummer. A text overlay highlighted Murphy’s comeback and Plummer's bronze medal win at age 30 in his Olympic debut, before the broadcast transitioned to the Women's 400m IM.

event_blocks.summary: In lane 4 of the Rio 2016 Olympics, Hungarian swimmer Katinka Hosszú, the favorite and a four-time Olympian seeking her first medal, prepared for the Women's 400m Individual Medley. She executed a powerful dive, transitioning seamlessly through freestyle, breaststroke, backstroke, and butterfly strokes. Dominating the race from above, Hosszú established a significant lead, notably pulling away during the 100m backstroke, with competitors like Maya Dirado (USA) and Emily Overholt (CAN) trailing by over 3 seconds at the 200m mark. The Omega timer displayed her split times as she approached the finish, where an on-screen update revealed her breaking the World Record with a time of 4:26.36, more than two seconds faster than the previous record, to the crowd's roaring cheers. Emerging from the pool visibly emotional, Hosszú was later seen on the victory podium, wearing a red 'HUNGARY' jacket and proudly holding her gold medal. She was flanked by silver medalist Maya Dirado (USA) and bronze medalist Mireia Belmonte García (ESP), both smiling under the Olympic rings. Text overlays emphasized her gold medal win after three previous Olympic Games and her commanding lead of at least 4 seconds. The segment concluded with a transition to the Women's 800m Freestyle, signaled by a title card over the pool, accompanied by uplifting, emotional background music featuring a female vocalist.

The commentator identifies her as 'the favorite' and notes she was competing in her 'fourth Olympic Games' having 'never won a medal' before, and that her world record time of '4 minutes 26.36 seconds was more than two seconds faster than the previous world record.'

event_blocks.summary: The Women's 800m Freestyle final at the Rio 2016 Olympics commenced with K. Ledecky (USA) in lane 4, S. Kohler (GER) in lane 1, L. Friis (DEN) in lane 2, J. Carlin (GBR) in lane 3, B. Kapas (HUN) in lane 5, L. Smith (USA) in lane 6, J. Ashwood (AUS) in lane 7, and M. Belmonte García (ESP) in lane 8 positioned on the starting blocks amidst a murmuring crowd. Following the official command "Take your mark" and a sharp electronic beep from the Omega timer, the race began with a synchronized dive, captured from an underwater angle, accompanied by loud cheering and splashing. K. Ledecky immediately established a significant lead, her stroke described as effortless and her body position exceptionally high in the water, contrasting with other swimmers. At the 50m mark, Ledecky's split was 28.03, ahead of the world record split of 28.35, with J. Carlin and S. Kohler trailing. By 100m, Ledecky maintained her lead with a 57.98 split, followed by Carlin at +1.91 and Belmonte García at a greater distance. Ledecky continued to extend her lead, reaching a two-body length advantage by 150m. Commentators highlighted Ledecky's exceptional technique, including her attention to detail on turns, a two-beat distance freestyle kick, and a streamlined hydrofoil-like movement, while noting other swimmers had parts of their backs above water. At the 200m mark, Ledecky held a 2.59-second lead over Carlin and a 3.20-second lead over Belmonte García, swimming under the world record pace. As she approached 250m, her time was 2:29.0 against a WR SPLIT of 2:31.03, extending her lead to over 3 seconds. By 300m, her lead surpassed 4 seconds, with the timer showing 3:01.80 against a 3:00.76 WR mark, prompting commentators to focus on the battle for silver and bronze. Ledecky's fast stroke rate, strength, and power-to-weight ratio were evident as she maintained her lead, with splits like 4:03.22 at 400m and 4:33.87 at 450m, consistently under world record pace. At 500m, her time was 5:02.94, still inside the WR split of 5:04.62, leading by 10 seconds. She continued to swim faster on subsequent laps, reaching 600m with a world record pace and 700m in 7:07.37, accelerating into a sprinter's kick with increased ratings and a quick head turn for breath. In the final stretch, Ledecky maintained her dominant lead, racing against herself and anticipating a new world record. She touched the wall at 8:04.79, breaking the previous World Record of 8:06.68, securing the gold medal, a new world record, and a new Olympic record. J. Carlin secured the silver medal for Great Britain, and B. Kapas took the bronze for Hungary, amidst an electric atmosphere fueled by the roaring crowd and bright Olympic lighting.

The commentator highlights her accelerating pace, stating she's 'consistently faster' and 'getting towards the last 100 meters,' 'really lifting it up' and going into a 'freestyle 100-meter sprinter's kick,' with 'the ratings increased' and a 'short sharp turn of the head to get the breath in.' Rio 2016 branding is visible on the pool walls and starting blocks. As Ledecky approaches the 700m mark, the Omega timer shows 7:05.44 with a -1.93 split advantage. A male commentator takes over, announcing 'a little over 100 to go' for the 'World Record holder, defending Olympic champion.' He observes her feet, noting the 'two-beat kick early on' and now 'plenty of splash there' as she 'staves off the world record line,' having 'well and truly beaten off the opposition,' making it 'her against the clock.' The male commentator confirms 'Kapash is still in second' and 'Jaz Carlin's got a fantastic battle for this silver medal.' The crowd's roar intensifies throughout, adding to the electric atmosphere as Ledecky continues her dominant, record-breaking swim.

The video captures the final stretch of the Women’s 800m Freestyle at the Rio 2016 Olympics, focusing on K. Ledecky (USA) in lane 4 as she powerfully dominates the race, with commentators noting she's "racing against herself" and anticipating a new world record. The overhead view shows the Olympic-sized pool with vibrant lane dividers, while the Omega timer tracks her progress, displaying splits like 7:37.84 at 750m, and a commentator highlights a "30.76" lap. Ledecky, wearing a black Speedo cap with 'LEDECKY' and a USA-branded swimsuit, maintains a commanding lead, with commentators emphasizing her distance from competitors, stating they "won't even be able to see the feet of Katie Ledecky." As she approaches the finish, the crowd's roar builds, with commentators confirming the "crowd urges her on." She crosses the finish line at 8:04.79, breaking the World Record of 8:06.68, which is immediately confirmed by on-screen graphics displaying 'NEW WR' and her name alongside the American flag, while commentators excitedly declare "Ledecky, gold medal! Ledecky, world record! Katie Ledecky of the USA reigns supreme yet again!" and confirm the time as "a new world record, a new Olympic record." A brief underwater shot captures Ledecky’s streamlined form and strong kick. Behind her, J. Carlin (GBR) in lane 3 and B. Kapas (HUN) in lane 5 trail, with commentators confirming Carlin wins the silver medal for Great Britain and Kapas the bronze for Hungary, also mentioning other trailing swimmers. The atmosphere is electric, with bright lighting illuminating the pool and the celebratory reactions of spectators, who are seen waving flags and recording the moment, their cheers erupting into a crescendo as Ledecky touches the wall.

event_blocks.summary: Following her dominant Women’s 800m Freestyle victory and world record of 8:04.79, K. Ledecky, visibly elated in the Rio 2016 Pool, was celebrated by commentators for her historic achievement of winning the 200m, 400m, and 800m freestyle events, a feat not seen since Debbie Meyer in 1968. The on-screen graphic confirmed her win, and a close-up showed her holding her gold medal amidst enthusiastic crowd cheers. At the Victory Ceremony, K. Ledecky stood on the top podium step, receiving her gold medal alongside Jazz Carlin (silver) and Boglarka Kapas (bronze), with commentators noting tears of joy. A text overlay highlighted her breaking her own world record by nearly 2 seconds and beating the field by at least 11 seconds, as upbeat music played. The scene then transitioned to the Men’s 100m Breaststroke final, introduced by a title card and a shot of swimmers, including those in AUS and USA caps, on the starting blocks. The race began with a loud crowd cheer and splash as swimmers dove into the Rio 2016 Pool. Commentators noted strong starts from C. VAN DER BURGH and K. CORDES, but quickly focused on A. PEATY in lane 4, who commentator 1 described as 'brilliant off the blocks' and 'flying' with a 'so fast' stroke rate. The Omega timer tracked Peaty's progress, showing him below world record pace at the 50-meter mark (26.61 seconds). Despite C. VAN DER BURGH's efforts, Peaty established a significant lead, described as 'three-quarters of a body length clear' with 25 meters remaining. A. PEATY ultimately won gold for Great Britain, setting a new world record of 57.13 seconds, a victory not achieved by Britain in this event since 1988. The crowd reaction was electric, with waving flags and visible excitement, underscoring the thrilling race and Peaty's dominance.

event_blocks.summary: Adam Peaty of Great Britain triumphed in the Men's 100m Breaststroke at the Rio 2016 pool, shattering the world record with a 57.13-second performance and securing a comfortable victory, celebrated with a Union Jack flag and '#TEAM PEATY' signs from cheering fans. At the subsequent victory ceremony, Peaty stood atop the podium, gold medal in hand, flanked by silver medalist Cameron van der Burgh of South Africa and bronze medalist Cody Miller of the United States, with on-screen graphics underscoring his record-breaking achievement as his third world record in the event and a margin of victory exceeding 1.5 seconds. Peaty expressed his resolve, stating, "All the hard work's actually paid off now and uh, no, we're not giving in now, we're gonna push it forward for the next year." The focus then shifted to the Women's 100m Freestyle, with swimmers including Pernille Oleksik, Sarah Sjostrom, Simone Manuel, Cate Campbell, Bronte Campbell, and Anya Weitzeil preparing on the starting blocks amidst an electric atmosphere, punctuated by whistle/buzzer sounds and dramatic rock music. During the race, Cate Campbell led at the 50m mark with a 24.77 split, followed closely by Bronte Campbell and Simone Manuel, who was identified as a significant threat. The race concluded with a surprising dead heat for gold between Simone Manuel and Penny Oleksik, both clocking 52.70 and setting a new Olympic Record, while Sarah Sjostrom secured bronze. Manuel, the first African American to win an individual Olympic swimming gold, and Oleksik, Canada's most decorated summer Olympian at 16 with her fourth medal, shared a moment of sportsmanship before their medal ceremony, accompanied by an upbeat pop song. The segment concluded by introducing the Men's 200m Butterfly final, featuring Chad Guy Bertrand Le Clos and Michael Phelps. In this event, Michael Phelps, after a close start where he was initially behind L. Cseh, quickly took the lead by the 50m mark and maintained it throughout, ultimately winning with an Olympic and World Record time of 1:53.36, reclaiming his title and eliciting a triumphant celebration amidst a midst the crowd' explosive roar of the crowd.

event_blocks.summary: The Men's 200m Butterfly final at the Rio 2016 Olympics commenced with an overhead view of swimmers, including Michael Phelps in lane 4, T. Pereira in lane 3, and R. Lochte in lane 5, diving into the Rio 2016 Pool and executing powerful butterfly strokes, with commentators noting Phelps' strong start and impressive underwater phase. As the Omega Timer tracked progress, showing Phelps's splits at 50m (24.74) and 100m (53.44), just 0.01 seconds behind the leader, commentators highlighted his 'company' and that he was 'right on the world record line,' with the crowd reacting with excitement. The camera alternated between overhead, side, and underwater views, capturing dynamic water splashes and swimmer technique, as commentators named the leading contenders: 'Phelps, Pereira, Lochte,' noting 'Brazil [is] looking for a medal.' In the final stretch, Phelps surged ahead, with on-screen text overlay confirming his 1st place at 150m (1:26.51), and commentators exclaimed, 'The mighty Michael Phelps! The champion is leading,' noting he was 'increasing the lead, increasing his stroke rate,' and declaring, 'There has never been a better swimmer. The most decorated swimmer is miles ahead!' The race concluded with Phelps winning gold, breaking the Olympic Record with a time of 1:54.00, followed by K. Hagino (Japan) in 2nd and S. Wang (China) in 3rd, as displayed on the finish screen, while spectators waved flags. The scene then transitioned to the victory ceremony where Phelps, wearing a red and navy USA jacket, received his gold medal and stood on the podium alongside Masato Sakai (silver) and Tamas Kenderesi (bronze), as upbeat electronic music played and text overlays highlighted Phelps reclaiming his signature event. The video then shifted to the Men's 200m IM event, with a title card displayed over the pool, and Phelps, in a navy USA jacket, walked toward the pool amidst crowd noise, with commentators exclaiming about his achievements. Swimmers, including Phelps and Ryan Lochte, prepared at the starting blocks, with the crowd chanting and commentators discussing Pereira's past achievements before the swimmers dove into the pool for the race.

The high-intensity Men's 200m Butterfly race at the Rio 2016 Olympics commences with the sharp sound of a starting gun, immediately followed by an underwater view of swimmers diving into the pool, their streamlined bodies cutting through the blue water. Commentators quickly highlight T. Pereira, exclaiming, "Now watch Pereira go," noting that "the crowd has got him pumped." The camera swiftly transitions to an overhead shot, showing the swimmers in their respective lanes, marked by vibrant dividers. Commentators identify Pereira, "third from your right, towards the top of the screen," as he edges slightly in front, swimming "alongside Phelps" and Lochte, confirming "they're all in a line." The Omega Timer displays the race progress, with Michael Phelps in lane 4 (black cap, 'PHELPS', American flag), T. Pereira in lane 3 (green cap, 'BRA'), and R. Lochte in lane 5 (black cap, 'LOCHTE'). At the 50m mark, on-screen graphics show Phelps with a split of 24.74, just 0.15 seconds behind the World Record, while Pereira trails by +0.17 and Lochte by +0.29 seconds. The commentators audibly read out "24.74" for Phelps, noting he's "lurking though," and that Pereira is "0.17 behind." Despite commentators mistakenly referring to "backstroke" and "breaststroke" at various points, the swimmers are clearly executing powerful butterfly strokes, creating splashes and bubbles. The crowd's excitement is palpable, with commentators stating, "The crowd will go crazy! They do!" The camera alternates between overhead and underwater views, capturing the intense competition and synchronized movements. Commentators also praise Phelps' "underwaters" and warn, "Don't write off Ryan Lochte," mentioning he's a "past champion." By the 100m mark, the timer reads 53.44, with Lochte now leading slightly, followed closely by Pereira and Phelps, all within 0.01 seconds of each other, swimming at "world record pace." The Rio 2016 branding is visible on the pool walls as the race remains tightly contested, setting the stage for a dramatic finish.

The high-intensity Men's 200m Butterfly race at the Rio 2016 Olympics begins with an overhead view of swimmers, including Michael Phelps in lane 4, T. Pereira in lane 3, and R. Lochte in lane 5, diving into the vibrant Rio 2016 Pool and executing powerful butterfly strokes. Two male commentators immediately note Phelps's strong start, despite a slightly wanting semi-final, praising his impressive underwater phase and powerful stroke, which they describe as 'working pretty well' for him in this Olympic campaign. As the Omega Timer tracks progress, showing Phelps's splits at 50m (24.74) and 100m (53.44), just 0.01 seconds behind the leader, the commentators highlight that he has 'company' and isn't easily shaking off competitors like Pereira, who is 'throwing everything at him.' They excitedly declare that Phelps is 'right on the world record line.' The camera alternates between overhead, side, and underwater views, capturing the dynamic water splashes and swimmers' technique. The atmosphere is electric, with the 'deafening' crowd reacting with excitement, especially as commentators name the leading contenders: 'Phelps, Pereira, Lochte,' noting 'Brazil [is] looking for a medal.' As the race progresses, Phelps maintains and then surges ahead, with the on-screen text overlay confirming his 1st place at 150m (1:26.51). In the final stretch, commentators exclaim, 'The mighty Michael Phelps! The champion is leading,' noting he is 'increasing the lead, increasing his stroke rate.' They declare, 'There has never been a better swimmer. The most decorated swimmer is miles ahead!' The race concludes with Phelps winning gold, breaking the Olympic Record with a time of 1:54.00, followed by K. Hagino (Japan) in 2nd and S. Wang (China) in 3rd, as displayed on the finish screen, while spectators wave flags and capture the triumphant moment.

event_blocks.summary: Following his Men's 200m Individual Medley victory at the Rio 2016 Pool, Michael Phelps, visibly exhausted, emerged to a roaring crowd, with the Omega Timer showing his winning time of 1:54.66 and a commentator noting his significant 1.95-second margin of victory, emphasizing his unprecedented fourth consecutive win in the event. The scene shifted to the victory ceremony where Phelps, wearing a navy USA jacket, received his gold medal alongside silver medalist Kosuke Hagino and bronze medalist Wang Shun, amidst cheers and an announcer's declaration. A text overlay highlighted Phelps' achievement as an upbeat instrumental track began. The focus then moved to the Men's 50m Freestyle final, with swimmers like N. Adrian in lane 6 preparing at the starting blocks under bright arena lights. After an official's call of "Take your marks" and the sharp sound of the starting gun, the race commenced with a synchronized dive. An underwater view captured the start, followed by an overhead shot tracking the swimmers, including A. Ervin in lane 3, F. Manaudou in lane 4, and N. Adrian in lane 6. Despite A. Ervin's early lead and the commentator's focus on him, N. Adrian surged in the final stretch to win, confirmed by an on-screen graphic. Adrian emerged from the water, raising his arms in triumph to the cheering crowd, including a fan waving a 'TEAM USA' flag.

event_blocks.summary: In the Men's 50m Freestyle at Rio 2016, Anthony Ervin, wearing a black swim cap with 'ERVIN' and an American flag, won gold in 21.40 seconds, narrowly beating French swimmer Florent Manaudou by 1/100th of a second, who secured the silver medal. Ervin, visibly emotional and celebrating in the pool, later stood on the podium in a navy USA jacket with red sleeves, waving to the crowd alongside Manaudou and a bronze medalist. Commentators highlighted Ervin's remarkable comeback, noting his return to the US National team after a period of personal struggle, and his achievement as the oldest swimmer to win individual Olympic gold, 16 years after his first win in the event. The narrative then shifted to the Men's 100m Butterfly, with Michael Phelps in lane 2, A. SADOVNIKOV in lane 1, J. SCHOOLING in lane 4, and C. LE CLOS in lane 5, poised at the starting blocks of the Rio 2016 Pool, with the Omega timer visible. The race commenced with a whistle and synchronized dives, with commentators immediately noting J. SCHOOLING's strong start and pace, while Michael Phelps' underwater performance was anticipated. At the 50m mark, A. SADOVNIKOV led, with J. SCHOOLING in fourth. In the final stretch, Joseph Schooling of Singapore surged ahead, securing gold with a new Olympic Record of 50.39 seconds, celebrated by roaring crowds and waving Singaporean flags. The podium ceremony for the 100m Butterfly recognized Schooling with gold, and a historic three-way tie for silver between Chad Guy Bertrand LE CLOS of South Africa, Michael Phelps of the USA, and Laszlo Cseh of Hungary, all receiving silver medals. The event concluded with a text overlay detailing Schooling's record and the unprecedented silver tie, before transitioning to a title card for the Men's 4 x 100m Medley Relay.

event_blocks.summary: The Men's 200m Breaststroke final at the Rio 2016 Olympics commenced with swimmers, including C. MILLER for USA in lane 5, executing powerful dives into the Rio 2016 Pool, accompanied by significant splashes and the audible 'BEEP' and 'BUZZ' of the start sequence. The Omega Timer tracked split times as commentators discussed race dynamics, noting Ryan Murphy's 'fantastic start' and 'big responsibility' to build a lead for C. MILLER, while also mentioning Walker Hebborn of Great Britain in lane 4. Underwater and overhead camera views revealed the pack, with Murphy and China in lane 2 leading at the 50m mark, Murphy ahead by 0.40 seconds, and Brazil in lane 1 in third. event_blocks.summary: The Men's 4 x 100m Medley Relay at the Rio 2016 Olympics culminated in a dramatic finish as the USA team, anchored by Nathan Adrian, secured a new Olympic Record with a time of 3:27.95. Adrian's strong anchor leg, following a dive that gave him a lead, propelled the USA past Great Britain's Duncan Scott, who took silver, and Australia's Kyle Chalmers, who earned bronze. The crowd's cheers were immense throughout the race, particularly as the USA surged ahead in the final 50 meters. Post-race, Michael Phelps, a key member of the victorious USA team, celebrated with his teammates, including Ryan Murphy, who contributed significantly with a 51.85 split. The team, later seen on the podium receiving gold medals, was hailed by commentators, with Phelps being called "the greatest Olympian." A female voice expressed the significance of being part of the relay team, especially as it might be a final appearance for some. The segment concluded with on-screen graphics detailing "Crazy splits" for various swimmers, including Ryan Murphy (51.85 WR), Adam Peaty (56.59), Kyle Chalmers (46.72), and Nathan Adrian (46.74), followed by a celebratory animation and calls to "THANKS FOR WATCHING" and "PLEASE LIKE & SUBSCRIBE."
